card.title: Quantum Entanglement
card.teaser: Two particles, lightyears apart, flip in perfect sync the moment you check one. Einstein hated it. Experiments confirmed it.
card.coverImagePrompt: Two glowing particles linked by threads of light across a dark starry void, connected despite vast distance, ethereal and scientific

lessons.title: Spooky action at a distance
lessons.body: Einstein called it 'spooky action at a distance' — and he meant that as an insult. Prepare two particles together, send one to Alice in Paris, the other to Bob on the moon. Measure Alice's particle. Bob's responds instantly, correlated in a way no classical physics allows. In 2022, three physicists won the Nobel Prize for proving this is how the universe actually works.

visual.imagePrompt: Two glowing orbs on opposite ends of a dark cosmic void, a shimmering thread of light stretched between them

lessons.title: What 'entangled' actually means
lessons.body: Take two photons born together, each free to spin one of two ways. Individually, either outcome is random. But their outcomes must cancel out — if one spins up, the other spins down. That's a correlation, not yet mysterious. The mystery is that the particles have no hidden instructions agreed in advance. The answer is decided only when you measure.

keyTerms.definition: A quantum state in which two or more particles must be described together — measuring one instantly affects the other
The idea that particles secretly carry predetermined outcomes; experiments have ruled this out

visual.imagePrompt: Two photons emitted from a central crystal, spiraling in opposite directions on a black background, scientific illustration

lessons.title: Bell's theorem — the cleverest idea you've never heard of
lessons.body: In 1964, physicist John Bell proposed a test: measure entangled particles along multiple angles, many times. If the particles had any classical 'hidden instruction', the correlations would stay within a specific mathematical limit. If quantum mechanics is right, they'd break that limit. Every experiment since has broken the limit. By a lot.
lessons.funFact: Bell's original paper was published in a tiny, short-lived journal that's now nearly impossible to find in libraries.

visual.imagePrompt: A chalkboard filled with mathematical inequalities, one inequality dramatically crossed out, chalk dust floating in lamplight

lessons.title: Closing every loophole
lessons.body: For decades, critics pointed to loopholes — maybe the detectors were biased, maybe signals could sneak through at light speed. So experimenters got creative. In 2015, teams in Delft, Vienna, and Colorado ran 'loophole-free' Bell tests. In 2018, physicists used light from distant quasars to pick measurement settings 7 billion years before the test. The correlations held. Entanglement is real, and it's not a trick.

visual.imagePrompt: A sophisticated laser setup with fiber-optic cables glowing through a darkened physics lab, equipment blinking

lessons.title: What it doesn't mean
lessons.body: Entanglement does NOT let you send messages faster than light. Alice's results look random no matter what Bob does — only when they compare notes (at ordinary speeds) does the correlation show up. The universe is nonlocal in a deep sense, but it is not telepathic. Physics remains weird enough without the sci-fi.

quiz.question: What did Bell's theorem show was impossible?
options: That quantum mechanics could be replicated by local hidden variables
quiz.explanation: Bell showed that no theory with local hidden variables can reproduce the predictions of quantum mechanics — and experiments back quantum mechanics.

quiz.question: Does entanglement allow faster-than-light communication?
options: Yes, but only for tiny messages
No, because each side's results look random on their own
quiz.explanation: The correlations only become visible when both parties compare data, which must happen via ordinary signals — no faster-than-light messaging.

quiz.question: What did the 2022 Nobel Prize in Physics recognize?
options: Loophole-closing Bell tests confirming quantum entanglement
The detection of the Higgs boson
quiz.explanation: Aspect, Clauser, and Zeilinger were awarded for their experiments showing entanglement defies classical explanations.

sources.title: On the Einstein Podolsky Rosen paradox
Loophole-free Bell inequality violation using electron spins separated by 1.3 kilometres
